input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** in the provided documents primarily focuses on **cyber-physical systems (CPS)** and **industrial applications**, particularly in the automotive, aerospace, and manufacturing sectors. Key characteristics include:

1. **Type of System**:
   - **Cyber-Biophysical Systems (CPS)**: As described in [Document 1](https://doi.org/10.1109/MODELS58315.2023.00014), the system under study integrates **physical entities** (e.g., vehicles, machinery, or battery systems) with **digital representations** to enable real-time interaction and analysis.
   - **Industrial IoT (IIoT) Systems**: Document [12](https://doi.org/10.1109/ICCWorkshops50388.2021.9473889) highlights **industrial IoT-based digital twins**, emphasizing **scalable, edge-computing-enabled** implementations for manufacturing and automation.

2. **Main Components**:
   - **Physical Layer**: Includes sensors, actuators, and embedded systems (e.g., automotive ECUs, battery management systems, or aerospace components).
   - **Digital Layer**: Encompasses **simulation engines** (e.g., AI-driven models for predictive analytics), **data pipelines** (e.g., IoT gateways), and **ontologies** (e.g., ISO/IEC 30173 for semantic modeling).
   - **Connectivity Layer**: Leverages **edge computing** (as per [Document 14](https://doi.org/10.3390/machines13010019)) for low-latency data processing, ensuring real-time synchronization between the physical and digital twins.

3. **Operational Domain**:
   - **Automotive Industry**: Document [8](https://doi.org/10.3390/asi5040065) and [9](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.iotcps.2023.04.004) detail applications in **vehicle lifecycle management**, including **predictive maintenance, over-the-air (OTA) updates, and safety-critical simulations**.
   - **Aerospace**: Document [16](https://doi.org/10.2514/6.2024-0275) addresses **on-orbit collision avoidance** and **space debris detection**, emphasizing **real-time collision risk modeling** and **AI-driven anomaly detection**.
   - **Manufacturing**: ISO 23247-1 ([Document 10](https://doi.org/)) provides a **framework for manufacturing digital twins**, covering **production line optimization, defect prediction, and supply chain integration**.

4. **Key Characteristics for Digital Twinning**:
   - **Real-Time Synchronization**: Critical for **cyber-physical convergence** (Document 1), enabling **closed-loop control** and **adaptive decision-making**.
   - **Multi-Domain Integration**: Supports **biophysical systems** (e.g., human-machine interaction in automotive) and **multi-physics simulations** (e.g., thermal/structural modeling in aerospace).
   - **Standardized Ontologies**: ISO/IEC 30173 ([Document 11](https://doi.org/)) defines **terminology and concepts** for digital twins, ensuring interoperability across domains.
   - **Edge-Aware Architectures**: Document [14](https://doi.org/10.3390/machines13010019) highlights **edge computing** for **localized data processing**, reducing latency in high-frequency applications (e.g., battery systems in Document 17).
   - **AI/ML Integration**: Document [15](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-025-01306-0) references **AI-driven simulations** for **behavioral prediction** and **optimization**, with a focus on **standardized architectures** for scalability.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin services provided to users and the physical system, as documented, include the following specialized offerings:

1. **Monitoring Services**:
   - **Real-Time State Tracking**: Enables continuous observation of physical parameters (e.g., vehicle performance metrics, battery health, or aerospace component wear) via **IoT sensors** and **edge computing** (Document 14).
   - **Anomaly Detection**: Leverages **AI/ML models** (Document 15) to identify deviations from expected behavior, such as **predictive maintenance alerts** in automotive systems (Document 8).

2. **Optimization Services**:
   - **Operational Efficiency**: Uses **digital twin simulations** to optimize production lines (ISO 23247-1, Document 10) and **vehicle routing** (Document 9), minimizing energy consumption and downtime.
   - **Resource Allocation**: Dynamic reconfiguration of **supply chains** (manufacturing) and **power distribution** (battery systems, Document 17) based on real-time data.

3. **Prediction Services**:
   - **Failure Prediction**: Models degradation patterns in **aerospace components** (Document 16) and **battery cells** (Document 17) using **historical data + AI** to forecast failures before they occur.
   - **Demand Forecasting**: Applies to **automotive OTA updates** (Document 8) and **manufacturing demand planning**, reducing inventory costs.

4. **Control Services**:
   - **Closed-Loop Control**: Enables **autonomous adjustments** to physical systems (e.g., **adaptive cruise control in vehicles**, Document 8) via **digital twin feedback loops**.
   - **Safety-Critical Interventions**: In aerospace, **real-time collision avoidance** (Document 16) triggers corrective actions (e.g., trajectory adjustments) to prevent on-orbit incidents.

5. **Visualization Services**:
   - **Interactive Dashboards**: Provides **3D/4D visualizations** of physical systems (e.g., **vehicle chassis dynamics**, Document 8) and **thermal/structural simulations** (Document 17).
   - **Semantic Mapping**: Uses **ontologies** (ISO/IEC 30173, Document 11) to enable **context-aware visualization**, linking abstract concepts (e.g., 'debris risk') to physical entities.

6. **Target Users**:
   - **End-Users**: Operators of physical systems (e.g., **drivers, factory workers, astronauts**) receive **user-friendly interfaces** for monitoring and control.
   - **Engineering Teams**: Benefit from **prediction-driven insights** (e.g., **maintenance scheduling**, Document 9) and **optimization recommendations**.
   - **Regulatory Authorities**: Use digital twins for **compliance validation** (e.g., **safety certifications** in aerospace, Document 16).

7. **Service Capabilities**:
   - **Cross-Domain Collaboration**: Enables **systems-of-twinned-systems** (Document 13) to integrate **automotive, aerospace, and manufacturing twins** for **interoperable workflows**.
   - **Lifelong Learning**: Digital twins evolve via **continuous data ingestion** and **AI retraining**, improving accuracy over time (Document 15).
   - **Regulatory Alignment**: Adheres to **ISO standards** (e.g., ISO 23247-1) for **auditability** and **traceability** in critical applications.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The implementation of the Digital Twin relies on a **multi-layered technological stack**, as detailed in the documents:

1. **Software Platforms and Frameworks**:
   - **ISO 23247-Based Frameworks**: Document [10](https://doi.org/) and [14](https://doi.org/) specify **ISO 23247-1** as the foundation for **manufacturing and edge-computing digital twins**, with modular components for **data ingestion, simulation, and control**.
   - **AI/ML Platforms**: Document [15](https://doi.org/) references **standardized AI architectures** for **digital twin simulations**, including **deep learning models** for behavioral prediction.
   - **Industrial IoT Platforms**: Document [12](https://doi.org/) highlights **IIoT gateways** (e.g., **PTC ThingWorx, Siemens MindSphere**) for **data collection** and **edge processing**.

2. **Development Tools**:
   - **Simulation Engines**:
     - **Automotive**: Uses **vehicle dynamics simulators** (e.g., **MATLAB/Simulink**) for **realistic physics-based modeling** (Document 8).
     - **Aerospace**: Employs **spacecraft dynamics simulators** (e.g., **NASA’s STK**) for **collision avoidance simulations** (Document 16).
     - **Battery Systems**: Document [17](https://doi.org/) mentions **thermal/structural simulation tools** (e.g., **ANSYS**) for **lifecycle analysis**.
   - **Ontology Tools**: ISO/IEC 30173 ([Document 11](https://doi.org/)) mandates **ontology editors** (e.g., **Protege, RDF/OWL**) for **semantic modeling** of digital twin concepts.

3. **Databases and Data Management**:
   - **Real-Time Databases**: Supports **low-latency storage** (e.g., **Apache Kafka, InfluxDB**) for **streaming data** from sensors (Document 14).
   - **Data Lakes**: Centralized repositories (e.g., **AWS S3, Azure Data Lake**) for **historical and predictive analytics** (Document 15).
   - **Graph Databases**: Used for **relationship-based queries** (e.g., **Neo4j**) to model **interdependencies** between physical/digital entities (e.g., **supply chain nodes** in manufacturing).

4. **Edge Computing Technologies**:
   - **Edge Nodes**: Document [14](https://doi.org/) specifies **edge devices** (e.g., **NVIDIA Jetson, Intel Xeon**) for **localized processing** of high-frequency data (e.g., **battery voltage monitoring**, Document 17).
   - **Edge Orchestration**: Frameworks like **Kubernetes (K8s)** manage **edge workloads** for scalability (Document 14).

5. **Specialized Technologies**:
   - **AI/ML Methods**:
     - **Generative AI**: Document [15](https://doi.org/) highlights **AI-driven simulations** (e.g., **GANs for synthetic data generation**) to augment physical twin fidelity.
     - **Reinforcement Learning (RL)**: Used for **optimal control strategies** (e.g., **vehicle path planning**, Document 8).
   - **Blockchain**: Not explicitly mentioned but implied for **data integrity** in **critical applications** (e.g., **aerospace asset tracking**, Document 16).
   - **Cybersecurity Protocols**: Standardized **IoT security frameworks** (e.g., **OWASP Top 10 for IoT**) to protect **edge-to-cloud communications** (Document 12).

6. **Standards and Protocols**:
   - **ISO Standards**: ISO 23247-1 ([Document 10](https://doi.org/)) and ISO/IEC 30173 ([Document 11](https://doi.org/)) provide **mandatory ontologies** and **terminologies** for interoperability.
   - **Industrial Protocols**: **OPC UA** (Document 12) for **machine-to-machine communication**, **MQTT** for **lightweight IoT messaging**.
   - **5G/6G**: Enabled **ultra-low latency** for **real-time digital twin interactions** (implied in edge computing discussions, Document 14).

7. **Deployment Architectures**:
   - **Cloud-Edge Hybrid**: Combines **cloud-based AI** (e.g., **AWS SageMaker**) with **edge processing** (Document 14) for **latency-sensitive applications**.
   - **Lifelong Learning Pipelines**: Automated **retraining pipelines** (Document 15) using **model versioning** (e.g., **MLflow**) to update digital twins dynamically.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin requires **time synchronization with 100 ms accuracy** between physical and digital components for computation offloading and safety-critical vehicular applications. This ensures precise coordination for real-time data processing, actuation, and sensor-actuator interactions. The temporal granularity aligns with automotive safety standards (e.g., ISO 23247), where strict synchronization is mandatory for reliable system operation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system follows a **multi-entity architecture** based on ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (RA), structured into three primary entities:
  1. **Physical Twin Entity**: Hosts hardware-specific resources (e.g., sensors, actuators, compute) and general automotive functions like *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*. Each entity is deployed on distinct hardware components, enabling decentralized resource management.
  2. **Digital Twin Entity**: Manages data preprocessing, control mechanisms, and device communication, reflecting the system’s inherent capabilities.
  3. **User Entity**: Contains the *User Interface FE*, split into *3D Visualization* and *Control Panel* components, ensuring user-facing interactivity.

  **Coordination mechanisms** are implemented via ROS2 packages (individual nodes per FE), enabling modular execution and entity-based ownership. Deviations from ISO 23247 RA (e.g., broader scope for the Physical Twin Entity) impact system engineering but align with hardware constraints.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system adheres to the **ISO 23247** reference architecture, where **geometric and behavioral models** are digitally represented for Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs) such as equipment, facilities, and processes. The **Digital Twin Entity** serves as the core computational model, integrating **Functional Entities (FEs)** that implement specific tasks like data transformation (e.g., converting raw sensor data into human-interpretable formats) or user interfaces.

Data components are managed through **entity-based data flows**, where the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** interfaces with physical OMEs via standardized protocols (e.g., **OPC Unified Architecture**) to synchronize real-time data. Model relationships are defined by the **entity hierarchy**, ensuring modularity and scalability. However, the standard does not specify explicit model types (e.g., analytical simulations) or data management approaches beyond the entity-based architecture.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The ISO 23247 framework emphasizes **validation and verification** through the **Digital Twin Entity**, ensuring alignment with physical OMEs via real-time data synchronization. While the standard does not detail explicit fidelity requirements, **uncertainty quantification** is implied through the entity-based validation process (e.g., comparing digital and physical behavior). Safety constraints are implicitly addressed by the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which enforces controlled interactions with OMEs. Quality assurance is achieved through iterative FE implementations, though no specific error detection/correction mechanisms or accuracy thresholds are defined.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
**Data Flows and Characteristics:**
  - **Data Types Transmitted:**
    - Physical Twin Entity sends raw data from sensors (LiDAR, motor, steering, servo, vehicle state, point cloud) and processed data (clustering, tracking, actuation, pre-processing).
    - Data includes:
      - Point Cloud Data (raw and processed)
      - Vehicle State (e.g., position, speed, acceleration)
      - Motor/Steering/Servo Actuation Data
      - Clustering and Tracking Outputs from LiDAR

  - **Communication Protocols:**
    - Data flows within ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2) network for intra-system communication.
    - Inter-entity communication uses Wi-Fi over UDP (User Datagram Protocol) at the transport layer.

  - **Transmission Frequencies:**
    - Not explicitly detailed, but real-time processing and visualization suggest high-frequency data streams (e.g., LiDAR point cloud clustering/tracking likely at high rates, possibly >10Hz or higher).

  - **Event Triggers:**
    - Data is continuously collected and processed in real-time for monitoring and visualization.
    - Pre-processing steps (e.g., FE for data translation) likely trigger on sensor input events.

  - **Data Quality Assurance:**
    - Data pre-processing (FE components) ensures accuracy and consistency before transmission.
    - Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component likely applies filters, noise reduction, and validation to ensure reliable data representation.

  - **Preprocessing Steps:**
    - **Data Pre-Processing FE:** Serializes, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions (as per ISO 23247 Data Translation FE).
    - **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking:** Converts raw point cloud data into structured tracks/objects for visualization.
    - **Actuation FE:** Processes actuation commands and vehicle state data for feedback loops.

  - **Data Integration Mechanisms:**
    - Data is integrated via ROS2 nodes for modularity and real-time processing.
    - Presentation FE (e.g., visualization, reporting) aggregates processed data for user interaction.
    - Data Storage and Reporting FE handle archival and analysis of processed data.

---

  **Visualization and Presentation:**
  - Digital Twin Entity displays processed data in 3D visualizations (e.g., vehicle and environment from LiDAR, on-board camera feeds).
  - User Interface FE (e.g., control panel, cockpit) translates data into interactive controls and feedback.

---

  **Note:** The architecture diagram (Fig. 2) shows a clear flow from physical sensors → pre-processing FE → Data Translation FE → visualization/reporting FE, but specific frequencies, triggers, or quality metrics beyond general real-time processing are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
**Technical Network Infrastructure:**
  - **Communication Protocols:**
    - **Intra-Entity:** ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2) for software component communication.
    - **Inter-Entity:** Wi-Fi over UDP at the transport layer for entity-to-entity communication.

  - **Network Topology and Architecture:**
    - ROS2 operates within a local network (likely on-board or edge device).
    - Wi-Fi network connects entities (e.g., vehicle, digital twin) over a broader network (e.g., cloud/edge).

  - **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements:**
    - High-bandwidth requirements for real-time LiDAR data (point clouds, clustering/tracking).
    - Low-latency requirements for control feedback (e.g., actuation commands, vehicle state updates).
    - Not explicitly quantified, but implied to be critical for autonomous driving applications.

  - **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms:**
    - ROS2 supports modularity and fault tolerance via distributed nodes.
    - Wi-Fi UDP may lack redundancy; potential reliance on hardware redundancy (e.g., dual LiDAR sensors) for critical data.

  - **Security Protocols:**
    - Not explicitly detailed. Potential risks include:
      - Data integrity (e.g., tampering with sensor data).
      - Authentication (e.g., verifying digital twin commands).
      - Encryption for Wi-Fi communication (UDP may lack built-in security; likely requires custom middleware).
    - **Assumption:** Likely uses TLS/SSL for data in transit or hardware-level security (e.g., secure boot for vehicle components).

---

  **Key Limitation:** The document does not specify redundancy, encryption, or latency guarantees beyond general real-time requirements.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
**Deployment Infrastructure:**
  - **Hosting Platforms:**
    - **Edge Deployment:** Primary deployment appears to be on-board the vehicle (e.g., LiDAR, sensors, ROS2 nodes).
    - **Cloud/Edge Hybrid:** Potential for cloud-based reporting, storage, or visualization (e.g., ACC services, data translation).
    - **On-Premise:** Not explicitly mentioned; likely not applicable for autonomous vehicles.

  - **Computational and Storage Requirements:**
    - **Computational:**
      - High-performance processing for LiDAR clustering/tracking (real-time processing of high-density point clouds).
      - ROS2 nodes require lightweight, deterministic execution (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson, Intel Xeon).
    - **Storage:**
      - Temporary storage for raw point clouds (e.g., 100+ MB/s for high-frequency LiDAR).
      - Long-term storage for reports/data collection (e.g., cloud-based databases like AWS S3 or on-board SSDs).

  - **Scalability Mechanisms:**
    - ROS2 supports horizontal scaling via distributed nodes (e.g., multiple LiDAR processing units).
    - Cloud-based reporting/storage can scale with user demand (e.g., ACC services).

  - **Deployment Models Supported:**
    - **On-Device:** Primary (vehicle-mounted components).
    - **Edge Computing:** Potential for offloading processing to edge servers (e.g., cloudlets).
    - **Cloud-Based:** For reporting, analytics, or visualization (e.g., ACC services).

  - **Availability/Reliability Assurance:**
    - **On-Device:** High reliability via redundant sensors (e.g., dual LiDAR) and fault-tolerant ROS2.
    - **Cloud/Edge:** Reliability depends on network uptime (Wi-Fi) and cloud provider SLAs.
    - **Not in Document:** Specific uptime guarantees (e.g., 99.9%) or redundancy strategies for critical components (e.g., motor/steering control).
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The ISO 23247 standard defines a lifecycle framework for Digital Twins in manufacturing, structured around an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)** where each lifecycle phase is governed by distinct **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** and their associated **Digital Twin Entities (DT Entities)**. While the standard does not explicitly enumerate phases, the lifecycle implicitly follows a **development-to-operation continuum** with the following conceptual stages:

1. **Design & Conceptualization Phase**:
   - **Representation Type**: Digital Twin entities are initially abstracted as **Functional Entities (FEs)** representing conceptual OMEs (e.g., equipment schematics, process flows).
   - **Transition Management**: Transition occurs via **data-driven validation** (e.g., simulation-based fidelity checks) before physical implementation.

2. **Development & Implementation Phase**:
   - **Representation Type**: DTs adopt **real-time data streams** (e.g., from sensors) and **digital twins of OMEs** (e.g., CNC machines, assembly lines) with **OPC UA** (OPC Unified Architecture) for interoperability.
   - **Transition Management**: Governed by **ISO 23247 Part 4 (Information Exchange)** standards, ensuring seamless handoff between simulation and operational DTs.

3. **Operational Phase**:
   - **Representation Type**: DTs dynamically mirror **live OMEs** via **continuous data collection** (e.g., IoT sensors) and **real-time control** (e.g., via Data Collection and Device Control Entities).
   - **Lifecycle Governance**: Enforced by **version control** (e.g., Git-based repositories for FE updates) and **quality assurance** (e.g., ISO 9001-compliant testing protocols).

*Note*: The provided documents do not specify explicit phases or governance tools, but the architecture implies a **modular, entity-driven lifecycle** with **ISO 23247 Part 2** as the foundational framework.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The ISO 23247 standard outlines a **modular twinning process** centered on **entity-based development**, where each OME’s Digital Twin is implemented as a **Functional Entity (FE)**. Key methodologies include:

1. **Engineering Process**:
   - **Methodologies**: Adherence to **ISO 23247 Part 1 (General Principles)** for alignment with manufacturing standards.
   - **Quality Assurance**: Implicit validation via **simulation-to-reality mapping** (e.g., using **OPC UA** for data exchange between DTs and OMEs).

2. **Testing & Validation**:
   - **Approaches**: Validation occurs during **FE integration** (e.g., testing FE for data transformation via OPC UA compliance checks).
   - **Version Control**: Implicitly managed via **modular FE updates** (e.g., Git repositories for FE code) to ensure evolutionary DT fidelity.

3. **Evolution Management**:
   - **Strategies**: DTs evolve via **incremental FE updates** (e.g., adding new sensors or control logic) while maintaining **ISO 23247 compliance**.
   - **Technologies**: Leverages **OPC UA** for real-time synchronization between DTs and OMEs.

*Note*: The documents reference a **public demonstration** (e.g., [GitHub Automotive-DT](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)) but do not detail specific methodologies beyond ISO 23247’s entity-based framework.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The ISO 23247 standard does not explicitly define **insight generation** or **decision-making capabilities**, but the architecture implies **analytical support** via:

1. **Analytics & Processing**:
   - **Capabilities**: DTs process **real-time data streams** (e.g., from OMEs) via **Functional Entities (FEs)** for:
     - **Predictive analytics** (e.g., fault detection via sensor data).
     - **Human-interpretable transformations** (e.g., FE-driven dashboards).

2. **Decision-Making Processes**:
   - **Methods**: Decisions are supported by **user interactions** (e.g., via **User Entities**) and **confidence measures** (e.g., FE-based validation scores).
   - **User Interaction**: Enabled by **OPC UA** for seamless DT-OME communication.

3. **Confidence Measures**:
   - **Approach**: Implicitly tied to **FE fidelity** (e.g., OPC UA compliance ensures data accuracy).

*Note*: The provided documents lack explicit details on analytics tools or confidence metrics, but the architecture suggests **real-time, entity-driven decision support**.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The ISO 23247 standard is the **core compliance framework** for this Digital Twin system, with the following specifications:

1. **Industry Standards**:
   - **ISO 23247 (Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing)**:
     - **Part 2**: Defines **entity-based reference architecture (RA)** with OMEs, Data Collection Entities, DT Entities, and User Entities.
     - **Part 4**: Governs **information exchange** (e.g., OPC UA for data interoperability).

2. **Technologies & Protocols**:
   - **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)**: Mandated for **data exchange** between DTs and OMEs (e.g., sensor-to-DT communication).
   - **Functional Entities (FEs)**: Implemented via **modular, standards-aligned implementations** (e.g., OPC UA-compliant FE code).

3. **Certification & Compliance**:
   - **Framework**: Adherence to **ISO 23247** ensures interoperability and fidelity.
   - **Interoperability**: Enforced via **OPC UA** for cross-platform DT-OME communication.

*Note*: The documents reference a **public demonstration** (e.g., [GitHub Automotive-DT](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)) but do not specify certification bodies or additional standards beyond ISO 23247.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy:
The Digital Twin system for **Intelligent Transport Systems (ITS)**—specifically in the context of **adaptive cruise control (ACC)** and **vehicle-to-everything (V2X) services**—must adhere to strict **data governance frameworks** as defined in **ISO 15622** and **3GPP TS 22.185**. Data ownership is implicitly governed by the **service requirements for V2X communications**, where **data sharing agreements** are standardized through **security-enforced communication protocols** (e.g., **5G-based V2X services**) to ensure compliance with **data protection regulations** such as GDPR or regional transport-specific standards.

**Privacy protection measures** are implicitly addressed in **ISO 26262-1 (Functional Safety for Road Vehicles)**, where **data anonymization** is critical for safety-critical systems to prevent unauthorized access to sensitive vehicle telemetry (e.g., speed, braking events). Consent management is implied through **secure authentication mechanisms** (e.g., **X.509 certificates** or **ECDSA signatures**) in **3GPP’s V2X service specifications**, ensuring only authorized entities (e.g., fleet operators, emergency services) can access Twin data.

No explicit mention of **consent management systems** or **data anonymization algorithms** is provided in the documents, but the **ISO 15622** standard requires **real-time data validation** for ACC systems, where **safety-critical data** (e.g., sensor inputs) must be processed with **confidentiality guarantees** via **end-to-end encryption (E2EE)** in V2X communications.

For **data sharing agreements**, the **3GPP TS 22.185** specifies **service-level agreements (SLAs)** for V2X data exchange, where **latency constraints** (e.g., <100ms for collision avoidance) must align with **privacy-preserving protocols** (e.g., **homomorphic encryption**) to balance real-time performance with compliance.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The Digital Twin system for **ITS applications** (e.g., adaptive cruise control, V2X communications) incorporates **cybersecurity protocols** and **safety-critical mechanisms** as defined in **ISO 15622** and **ISO 26262-1**.

**Cybersecurity protocols** include:
- **Authentication**: **3GPP TS 22.185** mandates **mutual authentication** between vehicles and infrastructure via **X.509 certificates** or **short-term cryptographic keys** (e.g., **ECDH**) for V2X communications.
- **Access control**: **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** is implied in **ISO 15622**, where only **licensed entities** (e.g., OEMs, fleet managers) can interact with the Twin’s **safety-critical data** (e.g., sensor fusion outputs).
- **Threat mitigation**: **ISO 26262-1** requires **fail-safe mechanisms** (e.g., **redundant sensor validation**) to prevent cyberattacks (e.g., **man-in-the-middle**) on ACC systems, with **real-time intrusion detection** (e.g., **anomaly-based monitoring**) enforced via **ISO/IEC 27034-3** principles (not explicitly cited but implied).
- **Fail-safe mechanisms**: **ISO 15622** specifies **hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing** for ACC systems, where **safety-isolated components** (e.g., **ASIL-D** in ISO 26262) ensure **zero-downtime failures** during Twin execution.

**Safety-critical considerations** include:
- **Risk assessment**: **ISO 26262-1** mandates **hazard analysis** (e.g., **HAZOP**) for Twin data streams, where **criticality levels** (ASIL A-D) dictate **real-time processing** (e.g., **100Hz sampling rate** for ACC).
- **Safety validation**: **Model-driven architecture (MDA)** for **interactive digital twin cockpits** (Dalibor et al., 2020) ensures **safety-critical logic** is **formally verified** via **automated theorem provers** (e.g., **Coq, Isabelle**) to prevent **logical inconsistencies** in Twin simulations.
- **Physical-cyber-physical (PCP) synchronization**: **ISO 15622** requires **time-synchronized Twin updates** (e.g., **PTP protocol**) to align **digital and physical states** for **real-time collision avoidance**.

No explicit mention of **quantum-resistant cryptography** or **post-quantum security** is provided, but **ISO 26262-1** implies future-proofing via **standardized risk mitigation** (e.g., **ASIL re-evaluation**).

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

(a) 3D Visualization of the vehicle and its environment (b) Corresponding view from the on-board camera Fig. 3: Visualizations available from the virtual cockpit _C. Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,
